coredata.dc:title: Various hydrophilic carbon dots doped high temperature proton exchange composite membranes based on polyvinylpyrrolidone and polyethersulfone
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Yu
Wang, Jin
Tao, Peipei
He, Ronghuan
coredata.dc:description: Novel organic-inorganic composite membranes were prepared conveniently by compositing of carbon dots (CDs) possessing different hydrophilicity into the low cost blended polymers of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and polyethersulfone (PES). The hydrophilicity of the CDs arises from its surface hydrophilic groups, which could be adjusted by controlling the reaction temperature and duration time. A series of homogeneous composite membranes doping with different hydrophilic CDs of up to about 10 wt% were obtained. Comprehensive characterizations were made in order to know the influence of different hydrophilic CDs on the properties of the prepared membranes. It is found that the doped CDs could cause the change in microphase separation and benefit proton conduction of the composite membranes. The more doped CDs, the higher the conductivity. A highest conductivity of 0.086 S cm−1 was reached by a composite membrane doped with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic CDs. Moreover, the incorporated CDs brought on the changes in properties of the composite membranes including free volume, hydrophilicity, acid doping level and swelling. A single fuel cell test was made based on the CDs blended membrane and indicating its potential to be used as the membrane electrolyte in high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite membrane
Carbon dots
Proton conductivity
Fuel cell
Mechanical property

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots with different hydrophilic degrees
Preparation of CDs doped PVP-PES composite membranes
Water uptake, acid doping and swelling of the membranes
Free volume and hydrophilicity of the membranes
Acid doping, swelling and mechanical properties of the membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) CD36.2° and (b) CD93.1°, (c) XRD patterns and (d) FT-IR spectra of the CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the PVP, CD36.2°, and the mixture of PVP-CD36.2° (1:1 in w/w) and PA-CD36.2° (10:1 in w/w).
SEM images (a, b, c and d) (surface, ×5000), and AFM images (e, f, g and h) of CDs doped composite membranes (the inserted photographs).
TGA curves of CDs, PVP-PES, and their composite membranes.
Fenton test results of PVP-PES and its composite membranes with CDs in 3 wt% H2O2 and 4 ppm Fe2+ at 68 °C.
Proton conductivity of acid doped membranes.
Schematic of proton transfer in PA doped PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membrane.

Polarization and power density curves of the fuel cell based on PA doped PVP-PES and PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membranes fueling with anhydrous H2/O2 at 150 °C, the Pt loading of the electrodes is 0.6 mg cm−2.
Free volume (Vfree ), water contact angles, water uptake and swellings of the membranes.

Acid doping level and swellings of the membranes (in 85 wt% PA at 45 °C for 24 h), proton conductivity at 180 °C and mechanical tensile properties at break at room temperature of the PA doped membranes.

head.ce:title: Various hydrophilic carbon dots doped high temperature proton exchange composite membranes based on polyvinylpyrrolidone and polyethersulfone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel organic-inorganic composite membranes were prepared conveniently by compositing of carbon dots (CDs) possessing different hydrophilicity into the low cost blended polymers of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and polyethersulfone (PES). The hydrophilicity of the CDs arises from its surface hydrophilic groups, which could be adjusted by controlling the reaction temperature and duration time. A series of homogeneous composite membranes doping with different hydrophilic CDs of up to about 10 wt% were obtained. Comprehensive characterizations were made in order to know the influence of different hydrophilic CDs on the properties of the prepared membranes. It is found that the doped CDs could cause the change in microphase separation and benefit proton conduction of the composite membranes. The more doped CDs, the higher the conductivity. A highest conductivity of 0.086 S cm−1 was reached by a composite membrane doped with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic CDs. Moreover, the incorporated CDs brought on the changes in properties of the composite membranes including free volume, hydrophilicity, acid doping level and swelling. A single fuel cell test was made based on the CDs blended membrane and indicating its potential to be used as the membrane electrolyte in high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) is one of the most highly efficient and sustainable power generation devices for transportation, stable and portable applications due to its high power density and low environmental impact [1]. The PEMFC working at a temperature above 100 °C has the advantages of enhanced tolerance of platinum catalyst to CO poisoning; accelerated electrode kinetics; simplification of water and thermal managements; as well as efficient heat recovery and utilization [1,2]. The proton exchange membrane (PEM) serves as electrolyte in the high temperature PEMFC (HT-PEMFC) should exhibit high proton conductivity under low relative humidity, possess high thermal and chemical stabilities, and have enough mechanical strength and compactness. To satisfy these requirements, many efforts have been made to improve the overall properties of the PEMs working at a temperature range from 100 °C to 200 °C [3,4]. Up to now, phosphoric acid (PA) doped polybenzimidazole (PBI) and its derivant based membranes have been widely investigated as they are regarded as the most promising high temperature PEMs [1,5–7]. However, the poor processability of the highly polymerized PBI and the toxicity of its synthetic raw material tetramine diphenylamine bring about the inconvenience for utilization of this polymer. Therefore, exploration of novel high temperature PEMs with superior properties is needed. The most popular proton donor is PA and this unique acid can provide high proton conductivity at temperatures above 100 °C even in the absence of water and has negligible volatility with low corrosion. Therefore, the alternative polymers generally should have high affinity to PA molecules for preparation of high temperature PEMs. Recently, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) blended membranes with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) [8] or polyethersulfone (PES) [9,10] have been developed as alternative HT-PEMs [8,11,12] due to their low cost and simple fabrication procedures with less environment effect. The N-heterocycle containing in the PVP allows the membrane to adsorb PA for proton conduction. However, PVP itself cannot be used as the PEM as it is water soluble. For fabrication the PEM with both high proton conductivity and superior durability, the PVP with an optimized content needs to be blended with other polymers for reasonable mechanical properties.

Inorganic fillers (SiO2, TiO2, Al2O3 and SnO2,) [13–19], metal-organic framework (MOF) [16,20,21], and nano-carbon materials (graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes) [1,2,22,23] has been proposed to prepare the composite membranes for improved properties in recent decades. In order to obtain a homogeneous composite membrane, the incorporated inorganic filler is generally prepared in nano scale and its surface is modified with various organic groups [24,25]. In recent years, quantum dots (QDs), has attracted much attention and been widely used in different fields. The QDs have intrinsic advantages including molecular level size (2–10 nm), high chemical stability, low toxicity and low cost for applications as nanocomposites [26–28]. Wang et al. [29] recently demonstrated a molecular-level hybridization of Nafion® using 2–5 nm quantum dots (QDs) as a new smart filler. The hydrophilic QDs in the ionic nanophase significantly increase the membrane proton conduction and the output-power of devices. It is known that hybridization of small-sized CDs [30] or QDs with varied hydrophilic or hydrophobic properties [31–37] could endow the hybrid materials with high performance [38]. Whereas QDs with varied hydrophilic properties are rare and seldom used for high temperature PEMs.

Herein, we synthesized CDs with a size of around 5 nm and exhibiting different hydrophilicity. The prepared CDs were used to prepare hybrid membranes and the superior specific properties of CDs are expected to achieve the PEMs high performances. The low cost polymer PES was used as the membrane matrix due to its excellent mechanical and chemical properties [9]. The N-heterocycle containing PVP, which is miscible with PES, was employed to adsorb PA molecules to construct the proton conducing system. In order to know the effect of different hydrophilic CDs on the performance of the membrane electrolyte, the molecular-level sized CDs were doped into the PVP-PES matrix and comprehensive characterizations were made to the CDs doped membranes.

Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (1300 kDa) and polyethersulfone (PES) (480 kDa) were purchased from J&K Scientific and Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid was bought from J&K Scientific. Ethidene diamine was purchased from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co. Ltd. China. All the chemicals are of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots with different hydrophilic degrees
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were prepared based on the literature [39]. The specific procedure is as follows. Ethylene diamine (0.335 mL) and citric acid (1.0507 g) were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL) in a teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL). The well-sealed autoclave was then heated at a certain temperature for different duration times to obtain CDs with different hydrophilicity. The product was purified via dialysis for 3 days. The different hydrophilic CDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying for 24 h. The obtained CDs are hereafter denoted as CDX, where X is the water contact angle of the product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs doped PVP-PES composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PVP and PES powders with a mass ratio of 1:2 (PVP to PES) were dissolved into 10 mL DMF and ultrasounded for half an hour at room temperature. A certain amount of CDs powders were added in the above solution at room temperature and ultrasounded until forming a uniform and transparent mixture. The membranes were fabricated by pouring the homologous mixture onto petri dishes and drying at 80 °C for 12 h. The resulted membranes were peeled off and washed thoroughly with deionized water and then dried at 45 °C. The blended membrane of PVP and PES, and their composited membranes with CDs are denoted as PVP-PES and PP-xCDX, respectively, where x is the mass percentage of CDX in the membrane.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of CDX powder and PVP-PES-based composite membranes were recorded with a Bruker VERTEX70 spectrometer. The crystalline structures of CDs and PVP-PES-based composite membranes were performed by an X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) using a PANalytical Empyrean (CuK 40 kV, 20 mA). The CDs were measured in a range of 10–70°, PVP-PES-based composite membranes were measured in the range of 5–30°. The morphology and particle size of CDX were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHIH-8100EM, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The surface and cross-section morphology of the PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membranes were acquired by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL 4800). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of CD36.2° and membranes were obtained using a DIMENSION ICON atomic force microscope. A contact angle goniometer (DSA 25, KRUSS, Germany) was used to measure the hydrophilicity of the CDs and membranes at room temperature. The stability of the samples was evaluated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TGA/DSC 3+) in N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Whilst all the samples were pre-heated at 100 °C for 24 h before TGA testing. Oxidative stability of the membranes was tested by immersing the membranes into 30 mL regularly refreshed Fenton reagent (H2O2, 3 wt%; Fe2+, 4 ppm) at 68 °C. To monitor the mass changes, the membrane samples were taken out, washed with water and weighted after drying. The mechanical strength of the membranes was measured by using the tensile strength instrument (CMT6502, SANS Company, China) with a separating rate of 5 mm min−1 at room temperature in ambient atmosphere. The free volume (Vfree ) of the membranes was determined using an automatic gas adsorption analyzer (Autosorb-IQ-MP-C), and N2 sorption and desorption were tested at 77 K and 453 K, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptake, acid doping and swelling of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The water uptake and acid doping level of the membranes were obtained according to the mass gain of the membrane samples after immersed in DI-water or 85 wt% PA solution at 45 °C for 24 h, respectively. The swelling of the membranes in area and volume by water and the acid, separately, was determined by comparing dimensional changes of the membranes during the water uptake or PA doping accordingly.

Conductivities (σ) of the membranes were measured by the four-probe method and with an alternative current frequency of about 2 kHz without humidifying. The PA doped membranes were pre-heated at 100 °C until a stable value of conductivity was reached. The proton conductivity was calculated using the following equation [40]:
(1)
σ =
L
R · d · w
where L (cm) is the distance of two electrodes for monitoring the resistance R (Ω) of the membrane samples, d (cm) and w (cm) are the thickness and width of the membranes, respectively.

The membrane-electrode assemblies (MEAs) with an active area of 4 cm2 were hot-pressed with a pressure of 2 MPa at 140 °C for 7 min. The gas diffusion electrode (provided by Danish Power Systems, Denmark) has a platinum loading of about 0.60 mg cm−2. Hydrogen and oxygen gases were supplied to the fuel cell at flow rates of 120 and 60 mL min−1, respectively, with no humidification according to the previous work [41]. Polarization curves were obtained using a current step potentiometry [42].

The organic functional groups on CDs, i.e., carbonization degree, were tailored by changing the reaction temperature and the reaction time (Fig. S1). The prepared CDs are thermally stable up to 200 °C according to the TGA curves (Fig. S2(a)). The obvious weight reduction occurred at temperatures from 200 to 460 °C. Morphologies of CDs were recorded by AFM (Fig. S2(b)) and TEM (Fig. 1 (a) and (b)). The results indicate that the prepared CDs with a size of about 2–5 nm dispersed uniformly without obvious aggregation. In addition, the broad diffraction peak centered at about 2θ = 24° in the XRD patterns (Fig. 1(c)) confirmed the amorphous nature of the CDs [39]. The highly hydrophilic CDs exhibited a stronger absorption at 1554 cm−1 by NH and a weak absorption at about 2900 cm−1 by saturated CH, respectively, due to the low reaction temperature and less reaction time as seen from Fig. 1(d). In contrast, the high reaction temperature brought on the highly carbonization lattice structure of CD93.1° because of the reduction of hydrophilic groups [29]. The changes in transmittance of the characteristic bands including CO, NH, CH, CNHC and epoxy groups are in good agreement with the hydrophilicity of CDs. In other words, corresponding to the increase in the carbonization degree of the CDs, a gradual increases in the absorption of CH and CO, and decreases in the absorption of NH, CNHC and epoxy groups are observed, respectively. Moreover, the contact angles with water of the CDs, shown in Fig. S1, confirmed the decreased hydrophilicity of CDs as the increase of carbonization degree.

FT-IR spectra of PVP, CD36.2° and the mixture of CD36.2° with PVP and PA, respectively, are shown in Fig. 2 . The sample of PVP-CD36.2° was prepared by drying the mixture of CD36.2° and PVP with a weight ratio of 1:1 in DMF. The characteristic absorption bands related to CD36.2° are observed at 1053 cm−1 (epoxy CO), 1670 cm−1 (carboxyl CO) and 1554 cm−1 (NH). The FT-IR spectrum of PVP with characteristic peak of CO at 1670 cm−1 and CN stretching vibration at 1090 cm−1 is observed [43]. Moreover, the characteristic adsorptions of the pyrrolidinyl groups at 1463 and 1421 cm−1 are also observed in the PVP spectrum. For the mixed PVP-CD36.2° sample, the peaks of epoxy CO (1053 cm−1) and NH stretching (1554 cm−1) decreased obviously. This variation indicates the formation of hydrogen bonding between the pyrrolidinyl groups of PVP and the functional groups on the CD36.2° surface [44,45]. As respect to the mixture of PA-CD36.2°, the obviously weakened absorptions of NH (1554 cm−1) and epoxy CO (1053 cm−1) are apparently caused by the interactions between the PA molecules and organic groups on the surface of CD36.2° via the hydrogen bonding.

The color of CDs doped PVP-PES membranes turns brown compared with the colorless pristine PVP-PES membrane as seen from the inserted photographs in Fig. 3 (a)–(d). The prepared PP-CDs membranes are uniform and dense, no obviously aggregated CDs particles and no pores are observed, indicating that CDs have been well dispersed in the PVP-PES matrix. The non-porous and compact composite membranes are expected for high open circuit voltage and low hydrogen and oxygen gas crossover during the fuel cell operation.

The AFM images of the composite membranes are shown as Fig. 3(e)–(h). The dark and bright areas represent hydrophilic ionic nanophase and hydrophobic backbone nanophase, respectively [46–49]. As expected that the doped CDs with higher hydrophilicity and more concentration resulted in more significant ionic nanophase of the composite membranes. For instance, the hydrophilic nanophase separation of the composite membranes of both PP-2CD36.2° and PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° is more obvious than those of the PP-10CD93.1° and PVP-PES membranes. The formation of the obvious ionic nanophase would benefit the ion conduction of the membrane.

The thermal stability of the membranes as well as CDs was measured by TGA and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . All the prepared membranes are stable up to 180 °C as seen in Fig. 4. Above this temperature, the mass loss of organic groups is observed to CDs and the composite membranes. The decomposition of PVP-PES main chain occurred when the temperature exceeded 400 °C. There are no obvious differences in the TGA curves between PVP-PES and PP-2CD36.2° due to the low content of CDs in the composite membrane. However, as the increase in the concentration of doped CDs, a slightly more mass loss is observed at about 200 °C for PP-10CD93.1° and PP-2CD36.2°/10 CD93.1° membranes. The results indicate that the prepared composite membranes can be used as the high-temperature proton exchange membrane.

Fenton test is a frequently used method for evaluation the chemical stability of the membranes under the attack of free radicals [50,51]. The mass retention of the membranes with the proceeding of the Fenton tests was recorded and shown in Fig. 5 . It is seen that the composite membranes doped with a small amount of CDs exhibit the similar mass decline to the pristine membrane. The degradation is mainly caused by the pyrrolidone in PVP since free radicals predominantly attack electron-rich aromatic compounds [1,42]. However, the membranes containing more CDs exhibit significant degradation. This phenomenon is probably resulted from the bigger free volume of the membrane by the more introduced CDs, which brings about more interspaces to expose the polymer chains under the attack of the free radicals (Fig. S6(a)–(d)). The free volume of the membranes will be shown in the next section in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free volume and hydrophilicity of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: It is seen from Table 1 that the hydrophilicity of the doped CDs has an obvious effect on the water contact angles of the composite membranes, and the effect increases as the increase in the doping percentage of the CDs. For instance, the 3% doping percent of CD36.2° brought about 8.8° deference in the water contact angle between the PP-3CD36.2° and PVP-PES membranes, and the presence of 10% CD93.1° resulted in a water contact angle of 84.1° for PP-10CD93.1° membrane. The results indicate that the hydrophilicity of the doped CDs could change the surface properties of the composite membranes.

The water uptake of the composite membranes is slightly increased as the increase in the incorporation percentage of the CDs. However, the slightly increased water uptake without resulting in increased swelling. A comparable or even a little small volume swelling is observed from the CDs doped membranes, which is probably caused by the increased free volume of membranes as seen from Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid doping, swelling and mechanical properties of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: It has been seen in Table 2 that membranes doped with small amount of CDs, e.g., PP-1CD36.2°, PP-1CD93.1° and PP-2CD36.2°/1CD93.1°, displayed comparably low acid doping level and correspondingly small swelling compared to the pristine PVP-PES membrane. However, as the increase of the CDs doping content, the PA doping level of the membrane increased but the swelling changed slightly. The higher acid doping level and less swelling of the composite membranes are apparently resulted from the presence of the CDs. We found that the doped CDs could decrease the crystallinity of the membrane main chain (Fig. S3) due to the weakened intermolecular forces of the polymer, which makes the easy PA doping in the membrane. As a support, the free volume of the composite membrane is significantly correlated to the amount of the CDs doped in the membrane as seen from Table 1. In addition, the highly hydrophilic CDs brought on discernibly high tensile stress at break and good flexibility of the composite membrane compared to the pristine one. For example, the PP-2CD36.2° membrane exhibited the tensile stress and elongation at break of 16.4 MPa and 89.8%, respectively, which are obviously higher than those of 12.7 MPa and 48.8% to the pristine membrane. The more organic functional groups on the surface of the highly hydrophilic CDs might be the cause.

The proton conductivity of PA doped composite membranes at a temperature of 180 °C without humidifying is shown in Fig. 6 and Table 2. It has been seen from Table 2 that the conductivity of the composite membranes is higher than that of the pristine PVP-PES membrane. The higher conductivity is mainly caused by the increased PA doping level due to the introduction of the CDs.

It is seen that the CD93.1° doped membranes have obviously high conductivity as the doping percentage of CD93.1° reached or higher than 5%. As a result, PP-10CD93.1° membrane exhibits a conductivity of 0.075 S cm−1, which is higher than that of 0.046 S cm−1 for PP-5CD47.7° membrane although they have almost the same PA doping level of around 140%. Moreover, PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membrane with a PA doping level of about 146% exhibits a highest proton conductivity of about 0.086 S cm−1 under identical conditions. This result is apparently caused by the presence of both CD36.2° and 10CD93.1° besides the slightly high PA doping level of the membrane. The hydrophobic CD93.1° located in backbone nanophase might afford an additional proton pathway for the increased proton conductivity [29] as schematically illustrated in Fig. 7 . In addition, the highly hydrophilic CD36.2° in the ionic nanophase could form relatively continuous channels (as seen in Fig. 3(h), Fig. S4(c) and (d)), which would further benefit the proton conduction. However, the introduced CDs content is limited due to the consistency between organic and inorganic components. It is seen that the conductivity of the membranes doped with 5% CD47.7° is almost the same as that of the membrane doped with 2% CD36.2° (about 0.045 S cm−1). In addition, when the doping percentage of highly hydrophilic CD36.2° exceeded 3% and CD47.7° exceeded 5%, the sedimentation of the CDs in the membrane could be observed with naked eyes.
The Arrhenius plot is shown in Fig. 6. The activation energy of about 17.3 kJ mol−1 is obtained according to the slope of the curves.

Fuel cell tests based on PA doped PVP-PES and PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membranes at 150 °C were performed with un-humidified hydrogen and oxygen. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . The performance of PA doped PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membrane based cell is better than that of the PA doped original one. The peak power density of the composite membrane based cell is 165.5 mW cm2 at 150 °C which is 47.0% higher than that of the pristine PVP-PES membrane based cell (112.6 mW cm2). The open circuit voltage (OCV) of PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membrane based cell is 0.97 V, which indicates the well compacted membrane with low gas permeability [52,53]. It is worthwhile to mention that the performance of the fuel cell based on the composite membrane by CDs is not superior since the used electrodes are commercial without optimization.

Novel PVP-PES based composite membranes with different hydrophilic nano-sized CDs were prepared and a highest doping percentage of 12 wt% was reached. The incorporation of the CDs brings on the changes in microphase separation of the membranes according to the AFM images. As results, the properties of the composite membranes such as free volume, hydrophilicity, acid doping level and swelling as well as conductivity are variously different from the original membrane. Among the composite membranes, the one containing both hydrophilic and hydrophobic CDs, i.e., PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1°, exhibits a highest conductivity of 0.086 S cm−1 at 180 °C without humidifying. A fuel cell test was made based on the PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membrane at 150 °C. The open circuit voltage of the cell is 0.97 V, indicating the well compact composite membrane. These results suggest that the CDs doped composite membranes are potential to be used as high temperature proton exchange membrane electrolyte in fuel cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51572044). Additionally, the authors also acknowledge the staff of Danish Power Systems (Denmark) for providing the gas diffusion electrodes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.06.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High temperature proton exchange membranes based on polybenzimidazoles for fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.F. Li, J.O. Jensen, R.F. Savinell, N.J. Bjerrum, High temperature proton exchange membranes based on polybenzimidazoles for fuel cells, Progress in Polymer Science 34(5) (2009) 449-477.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of high-temperature polymer electrolyte membrane fuel-cell (HT-PEMFC)-based auxiliary power units for diesel-powered road vehicles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Liu, W. Lehnert, H. Janssen, R.C. Samsun, D. Stolten, A review of high-temperature polymer electrolyte membrane fuel-cell (HT-PEMFC)-based auxiliary power units for diesel-powered road vehicles, Journal of Power Sources 311 (2016) 91-102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer and composite membranes for proton-conducting, high-temperature fuel cells: a critical review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Quartarone, S. Angioni, P. Mustarelli, Polymer and Composite Membranes for Proton-Conducting, High-Temperature Fuel Cells: A Critical Review, Materials 10(7) (2017).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving the overall characteristics of proton exchange membranes via nanophase separation technologies: a progress review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hu, X. Li, L. Yan, B. Yue, Improving the Overall Characteristics of Proton Exchange Membranes via Nanophase Separation Technologies: A Progress Review, Fuel Cells 17(1) (2017) 3-17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Acid doped polybenzimidazoles based membrane electrode assembly for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cell: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Haque, A.B. Sulong, K.S. Loh, E.H. Majlan, T. Husaini, R.E. Rosli, Acid doped polybenzimidazoles based membrane electrode assembly for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cell: A review, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy 42(14) (2017) 9156-9179.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review of PBI-based high temperature PEM fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Araya, F. Zhou, V. Liso, S.L. Sahlin, J.R. Vang, S. Thomas, X. Gao, C. Jeppesen, S.K. Kaer, A comprehensive review of PBI-based high temperature PEM fuel cells, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy 41(46) (2016) 21310-21344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in polybenzimidazole/phosphoric acid membranes for high-temperature fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Subianto, Recent advances in polybenzimidazole/phosphoric acid membranes for high-temperature fuel cells, Polymer International 63(7) (2014) 1134-1144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New anhydrous proton exchange membranes for high-temperature fuel cells based on PVDF-PVP blended polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.B. Guo, X. Xu, Y. Xiang, S.F. Lu, S.P. Jiang, New anhydrous proton exchange membranes for high-temperature fuel cells based on PVDF-PVP blended polymers, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 3(1) (2015) 148-155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel phosphoric acid doped poly(ethersulphone)-poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) blend membrane for high-temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, H.N. Wang, S.F. Lu, Z.B. Guo, S.Y. Rao, R.J. Xiu, Y. Xiang, A novel phosphoric acid doped poly(ethersulphone)-poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) blend membrane for high-temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells, Journal of Power Sources 286 (2015) 458-463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Submicro-pore containing poly(ether sulfones)/polyvinylpyrrolidone membranes for high-temperature fuel cell applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.B. Guo, R.J. Xiu, S.F. Lu, X. Xu, S.C. Yang, Y. Xiang, Submicro-pore containing poly(ether sulfones)/polyvinylpyrrolidone membranes for high-temperature fuel cell applications, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 3(16) (2015) 8847-8854.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Polym. Sci. Part B – Polym. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Bozkurt, W.H. Meyer, Proton-conducting poly(vinylpyrrolidon)-polyphosphoric acid blends, Journal of Polymer Science Part B-Polymer Physics 39(17) (2001) 1987-1994.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New highly proton-conducting membrane poly(vinylpyrrolidone)(PVP) modified poly(vinyl alcohol)/2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propanesulfonic acid (PVA-PAMPS) for low temperature direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Qiao, T. Hamaya, T. Okada, New highly proton-conducting membrane poly(vinylpyrrolidone)(PVP) modified poly(vinyl alcohol)/2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propanesulfonic acid (PVA-PAMPS) for low temperature direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs), Polymer 46(24) (2005) 10809-10816.
